mnemonics.title: Page Fault Handling Steps
mnemonics.content: SAFA: Stall instruction, Find page on disk, Allocate frame, Access completed. First check Valid bit, then Search disk, then Find free frame, then Add to memory, then Restart instruction.

mnemonics.title: Page Table Entry Fields
mnemonics.content: FURDP: Frame number, Use bit (reference), Reserved, Dirty bit, Protection bits. HELPS: Hardware, Error handling, Location, Protection, Sharing.

mnemonics.title: FIFO Page Replacement
mnemonics.content: FIRST IN FIRST OUT - The page that entered memory first leaves first, like a queue at a ticket counter.

acronyms.usage: Hardware cache for virtual-to-physical address translations
Page replacement algorithm evicting oldest accessed page
Page replacement removing oldest page in memory
Memory management technique using disk as extension of RAM
Memory architecture with different access times for different locations

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flowchart: CPU requests address → Check TLB → Miss → Check Page Table → Valid bit set? → No (Page Fault) → OS finds page on disk → Allocates frame → Reads from disk → Updates page table → Restarts instruction

Split virtual address into Page Number + Offset. Use Page Number to index page table, get Frame Number. Combine Frame Number with Offset to get Physical Address. Use a simple diagram showing virtual page → page table → physical frame mapping.
Draw a loop: High page faults → Process spends time paging I/O → Less work done → More page faults → More paging I/O. Show CPU utilization dropping dramatically.
Imagine pages arranged in a circular buffer (clock face). A pointer scans pages; if reference bit=0, replace; if=1, clear bit and move on. Continues until finding a page to evict.

analogies.analogy: Like a library's book reservation system. You can request books you don't have in your personal shelf (memory). The library (OS) brings books from the main storage (disk) to your shelf when needed, even if your shelf seems full.
Like searching for a book in a library that you have on your borrowing list but haven't picked up yet. You need to go to the shelf (load from disk) before you can read it.
Like a dictionary's index tabs you use frequently. Instead of flipping through every page, you remember where certain words are located for quick access.
Like a student who spends all their time going to the library (fetching pages) instead of actually studying (computing). The constant trips back and forth leave no time for actual work.
Like the open books on your desk while studying. These are the ones you actively use; books in your bookshelf (disk) are there but not currently needed.
Like two people reading the same book. Both can read simultaneously without disturbance. Only when one wants to write notes does the person make their own personal copy.
